title: vLLM Serving Routes with NPU-Aware vLLM Are Zero-Operator Migration
problem_description: When migrating a project that uses vLLM for serving, and vLLM is already pre-built for Ascend NPU (imported from /vllm-workspace/vllm/ with ascend platform plugin), the Phase 5 error analyzer may dispatch operator-level fixes unnecessarily. In this MinerU migration, the only error was a validation script port mismatch — no CUDA operators existed in the serving code path. The error analyzer correctly classified the error as 'validation' and dispatched to 'code_adapter', but future runs could waste iterations exploring non-existent operator incompatibilities if the analyzer incorrectly classifies a configuration-only failure as operator-related.
rough_fix_approach: Before dispatching operator-level fixes for vLLM serving route errors, first verify: (1) vLLM is imported from an NPU-aware build (check import path for ascend/npu markers), and (2) the launch command succeeds in starting the server on NPU (ascend platform plugin loaded, NPU blocks allocated). If both are true, treat the error as configuration/validation rather than operator incompatibility. Short-circuit the operator_fixer path and route directly to code_adapter for config adjustments (port, environment variables, file paths). This prevents unnecessary Phase 5 iterations exploring operator-level fixes that don't exist.
artifact_evidence: raw/phase_5_validation_attempt0.json: error_category='validation', fix_operator.summary='No operator incompatibility found'
raw/phase_fix_operator_attempt0.json: modified_files=[], error_classification='validation (port mismatch, not operator incompatibility)'

involved_code_files.role: Generated validation entry script — DEFAULT_SERVING_PORT constant mismatched server's hardcoded port
vLLM server entry point — hardcodes port 30000 without --port flag exposure

reasoning: This experience is highly generalizable because vLLM serving is a common migration route for LLM/VLM projects. When vLLM is pre-built for Ascend NPU (as it was here at /vllm-workspace/vllm/), the serving path contains zero CUDA-specific operators — vLLM's ascend platform plugin handles all device abstraction. The MinerU code never calls torch.cuda or any CUDA-specific API in its vLLM serving path. The validated evidence shows the server ran on Ascend NPU natively with no CPU fallback and returned real model output (MinerU2.5-Pro-2605-1.2B, 8 tokens). The fix operator correctly identified zero operator incompatibilities. Capturing this as a rule allows future vLLM serving migrations to short-circuit unnecessary operator exploration, potentially saving 2-3 Phase 5 iterations. Confidence is 0.85 rather than higher because this depends on vLLM being pre-built for NPU — not all environments will have this configuration.
